title: AI & the Future of Software Engineering
author: Anil Sharma
text:
AI & the Future of Software Engineering
(How AI Will Empower Software Professionals, Not Replace Them)
If you’re an architect, a developer, a product manager, or an engineering lead, you’ve probably felt this. That mix of excitement and dread when you watch AI generate in seconds what used to take you hours. The quiet worry that maybe you’re witnessing your own replacement.
It is a valid fear. The speed at which AI operates can feel overwhelming. As a software company, we are not immune to this uncertainty; we have faced the same dilemma and asked the same hard questions.
So let me give you a direct answer: No. AI will not make you obsolete. But it will change how you work — profoundly. Those who adopt AI-powered software engineering will innovate faster, deliver higher quality, and explore domains beyond their traditional expertise.
To understand this change, we must look at history:
When the wheel was invented, the powerful men of the community, pulling rocks uphill, were concerned. Soon they were wheeling more rocks and building castles.
We are currently at that “wheel” moment.
What Does This Change Look Like?
Let’s move from theory to reality. Consider the architecture diagram below.
This is the complete, HIPAA-compliant Cloud Architecture generated from a single sentence: “I want to build a hospital management application that is HIPAA compliant”
In a traditional workflow, reaching this level of clarity — defining the Presentation Layer, API Layer, and Application Services Layer would take a team of experts days or weeks of white boarding. With AI, you can generate this baseline in seconds.
This delivers immediate gratification. It eliminates the intimidation of the blank page and lets you start editing immediately.
But There’s a Catch
The power of AI comes with a dangerous caveat. That architecture diagram above looks perfect, but if generated without rigor, it might just be a pretty hallucination.
If you feed an AI a massive, undefined request, like “build me a hospital app”, the context becomes too vast. The AI loses the plot. Instead of converging on a workable solution, it diverges, roaming in a zigzag direction.
Think of it this way: It is the difference between asking an architect to “design a building” versus asking them to “draft the foyer dimensions based on this specific occupancy code.” AI fails at the former (the broad, vague request) but excels at the latter (the specific, constrained task).
To turn that “pretty diagram” into working, reliable software, we cannot just chat with the bot. We need a process.
The Solution: The AI-Powered SDLC
Traditional SDLC failed due to a lack of rigor simply because maintaining that rigor was too expensive, time-consuming, and often humanly impossible. We must apply the rigor of a Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC), but one reimagined for the AI age.
To make AI effective, we must break the problem space into small, managed steps orchestrated by an SDLC workflow. This workflow moves sequentially through distinct phases to lock in the context and prevent drift:
- Requirements: Integrating ideas and user stories.
- Specifications: Defining user scenarios and architecture.
- Design: Creating data models and API definitions.
- Code Generation: Artifacts produced by AI-Powered SDLC platform (such as Trillo AI) and Claude Code can generate working code of an application. The blog Architecting Cloud Applications with AI covers this topic in detail.
By breaking the project down, we ensure the AI is never “guessing” about the whole world. It is only solving one specific puzzle at a time.
The rest of this blog walks through a few phases. But first, let’s address the most important question: Where do you fit in this process?
You Are the Architect, Not the Passenger
Automation does not mean losing control. In this model, AI is the engine but you are the Chief Architect.
Your role is to guide the AI toward convergence: reviewing outputs, catching blind spots, enforcing constraints the AI doesn’t know about. The tool’s job is to make that guidance easy, visual, and intuitive.
We call this the Human-in-the-Loop workflow. Let’s see it in action.
SDLC Step 1 — Generating the Software Specification with Human-in-the-Loop
The SDLC begins with Requirements. These can range from a one-liner to a hundred-page RFP. Your first goal is to convert that raw input into a structured Software Specification.
Here’s how the Human-in-the-Loop workflow operates at this stage:
1. Input & Generation You provide the requirements, and the AI generates a baseline draft. This isn’t just a summary, it’s a comprehensive specification including user personas, functional/non-functional requirements, data entities, workflows, integrations, and Cloud Service requirements.
2. The Review Loop This is where you take the wheel. You review the baseline and choose one of four paths:
- Clarify: Use the sidebar conversation (like Google Docs comments) to ask the AI questions about dense sections. It answers using the specification as its locked context.
- Refine: Use UI or instruct the AI to adjust, add, or delete content incrementally.
- Regenerate: If the draft missed the mark, note your constraints and regenerate from scratch.
- Revise Input: If outputs are consistently off, the source requirements are likely too vague. Revise them and restart.
The Result: Convergence in Hours, Not Weeks
Based on our case studies, an architect can reach a highly accurate Software Specification in a few iterations, typically less than a day.
This offers a strategic advantage: because this step often happens before a project is funded, generating high-fidelity specifications instantly lets you present a mature proposal to stakeholders without weeks of unpaid work.
The following screenshots show this workflow in practice. Notice the sidebar conversation on the right. This is your cockpit for steering the AI.
SDLC Step 2 — Generate Architecture Diagram using AI
With the specification locked in, the next phase is architecture.
Here, AI uses the Software Specification as context to generate a system architecture diagram. If you’ve refined the specification to high accuracy in the previous step, the generated architecture will reflect that accuracy. But it may not match your vision exactly.
This is expected. Note your feedback, supply it as input, and regenerate. The AI will adjust.
Regenerate Dialog
Converging on the architecture typically takes two or three regeneration cycles with comments. During regeneration, you can choose to retain past execution history as context — useful for incremental refinements where you want to preserve earlier decisions.
In other cases, you may want to start fresh. Uncheck “Retain Past Execution Memory as Reference” to discard prior context and regenerate from a clean slate.
Rest of Activities and Their Organization
The diagram below shows all activities in the SDLC pipeline. They appear in the logical order they’re executed, moving from specification through design to prototype. We will cover them in future blogs.
The activities are classified in three categories (progressively including previous perspectives).
Specification Perspective Activities
This phase transforms raw requirements into structured artifacts: the Software Specification, Architecture, Estimates, and Cloud Service mappings. Each artifact feeds the next, building context as you progress.
Design Perspective Activities
With specifications locked, the Design phase produces the detailed blueprints: data models, API definitions, UI structures, workflows, and external system integrations. These become the source of truth for code generation.
Prototype Perspective Activities
This phase generates working software: test data, UI code, and a deployable prototype on cloud infrastructure. What would traditionally take weeks of development happens in hours.
Holistic Analysis Feature
At any point, you can run a cross-artifact analysis to surface inconsistencies, gaps, or misalignments between phases. Think of it as a built-in reviewer that catches what human eyes might miss.
In Trillo AI, this feature is accessible via the green ‘Analysis’ button on the top bar.
Concluding Remarks
We started with a fear: that AI might make us obsolete. We end it with a realization: AI is the lever that allows us to lift heavier rocks.
By adopting this AI-powered process, you are stepping up. You are moving away from the fatigue of “blank page syndrome” and the drudgery of manual drafting. You are embracing a role where you innovate faster, deliver higher quality, and explore areas beyond your traditional expertise.
The future of software engineering isn’t about writing code from scratch. It is about orchestration, judgment, and vision using platforms that support rigors of software engineering.
It is time to stop pulling rocks and start building castles.
